metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step (or AC item) to enable GitHub's 'Private vulnerability reporting' repository setting (Settings → Security → Advisories → Private vulnerability reporting → Enable), so the primary channel advertised in SECURITY.md is actually functional.
findings.finding: The plan advertises GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting as the *primary* disclosure channel in SECURITY.md, but this feature requires a GitHub repository setting ('Private vulnerability reporting' under Settings → Security → Advisories) to be enabled — the SECURITY.md file alone does not activate it. If the setting is off, the 'Report a vulnerability' button is absent or non-functional, making the primary channel described in the file a dead end for reporters.
findings.location: What section — SECURITY.md description; AC line 1 ('GitHub private vuln reporting as the primary channel')
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line: '[ ] GitHub Private Vulnerability Reporting is enabled in the repository settings (Settings → Security → Advisories) so the "Report a vulnerability" button is visible and functional to external contributors.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC line requiring explicit confirmation (e.g., a maintainer sign-off in the PR or a tested email round-trip) that `hello@navapbc.com` routes to an actively monitored inbox before the files are merged.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `hello@navapbc.com` is used 'per maintainer decision' as the enforcement/fallback contact for SECURITY.md and CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md, but this email address does not appear anywhere in the codebase. The only navapbc.com address in the repo is `joeoakhart@navapbc.com`. The plan's own Scope section acknowledges the email 'should be confirmed to route before the first public release,' but there is no AC line that requires this confirmation to happen.
findings.location: What section / Scope section; AC for SECURITY.md and CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that verifies the code comment about confirming `hello@navapbc.com` routing exists in at least one of the affected files (e.g., SECURITY.md or CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md).
findings.finding: The Scope section specifies a concrete deliverable — a code comment noting that `hello@navapbc.com` should be confirmed to route correctly before the first public release — but no acceptance criterion covers or verifies this deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line such as: '[ ] At least one affected file contains an inline comment (e.g., HTML comment or YAML `#` comment) reminding maintainers to verify `hello@navapbc.com` routes correctly before public release.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether GitHub Issues is currently enabled on navapbc/rebar (check mirror-lock status), and either: (a) reconcile SUPPORT.md to not direct users to GitHub Issues if they are disabled, or (b) explicitly note in the plan that re-enabling Issues is a prerequisite and update the mirror-lock runbook to reflect the deliberate decision to keep Issues on.
findings.finding: The plan's SUPPORT.md spec says 'issues for bugs/features' will be a valid channel, but infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md explicitly disables GitHub Issues (`gh api -X PATCH repos/navapbc/rebar -F has_issues=false`) as part of mirror-lock hygiene. If issues are disabled on the GitHub mirror, the SUPPORT.md content directing users there is misleading or dead on arrival.
findings.location: What section / SUPPORT.md bullet: 'issues for bugs/features'; Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prerequisite check: confirm `gh api /repos/navapbc/rebar --jq '.has_issues'` returns `true`. If false, either update SUPPORT.md to route bug reports through a different channel (e.g., Gerrit or a mailing list), or add a step to re-enable Issues and update `infra/runbooks/github-mirror-lock.md` to not disable them at cutover.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete verification step for issue form validity — e.g., validate each `.yml` against the GitHub issue-form schema (required keys: `name`, `description`, `body` array) via a linter or by loading the template URL in the GitHub UI after push, and include this as part of AC #3's definition of done.
findings.finding: AC #3 specifies that the issue template directory 'has bug + feature issue forms' but no step verifies the YAML is valid GitHub issue-form syntax. GitHub silently ignores malformed issue form YAML, so the files could exist and pass a file-existence check while never rendering correctly in the GitHub issue creation UI — creating a gap between 'what we'll do' (create files) and 'what done looks like' (contributors actually see structured forms).
findings.suggested_fix: After pushing, verify each template renders in the GitHub issue creation UI (navigate to `github.com/<org>/<repo>/issues/new/choose`) and confirm the structured form fields appear. Alternatively, add a CI step using `check-jsonschema` with the GitHub issue-form schema to validate YAML before merge.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `CONTRIBUTING.md` exists and already documents the Gerrit submission flow, OR add an AC / task to create/update it before merging these community health files.
findings.finding: The new files (PR template, issue config.yml, SUPPORT.md) all reference `CONTRIBUTING.md` as the authoritative guide to the Gerrit flow, but the plan never confirms that `CONTRIBUTING.md` already exists and already covers the Gerrit submission process. If it is absent or silent on Gerrit, every referral link in the new files will mislead contributors.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit AC item: 'CONTRIBUTING.md contains step-by-step Gerrit submission instructions and is reachable from the GitHub UI.' If it does not yet exist, add its creation to this plan's scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the 'Gerrit-as-merge-gate / GitHub-mirror-with-redirect' architectural decision in an ADR, a CLAUDE.md note, or a dedicated section of CONTRIBUTING.md so the rationale is discoverable independently of the PR template.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate architectural/workflow decision — GitHub PRs will never be merged (Gerrit is the canonical merge gate) and a mirror bot is explicitly out of scope — but does not propose capturing this decision in an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or CONTRIBUTING.md. The rationale lives only inside the PR template body, which is invisible to maintainers who never open the template file directly.
findings.location: Scope section: 'The redirect PR template is the deliberate substitute for the out-of-scope PR->Gerrit mirror bot'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR (e.g., `docs/adr/0001-gerrit-merge-gate.md`) or a 'How this repo works' section in CONTRIBUTING.md explaining: GitHub is a read-only mirror, PRs are redirected to Gerrit, a mirror bot was considered and deferred, and the reasons why.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
